subject: mathematics
chapter_title: Predicting What Comes Next: Exploring Sequences and Progressions

core_competencies.justification: The chapter spends its first three sections building the student's ability to read and analyse a sequence as a rule that generates terms one after another, distinguishing an explicit rule that computes any term straight from its position from a recursive rule that builds each term out of the one or two terms before it, including the Virahanka-Fibonacci rule that depends on two prior terms at once.

Once arithmetic and geometric progressions are introduced, the chapter repeatedly asks the student to work out the explicit and recursive formula that produces a given growing pattern, from the stage-by-stage square patterns and the Sierpinski triangle and square carpet to the bouncing-ball and taxi-fare scenarios, each time turning a description of how a quantity grows into a general procedure for finding any term.

adjunct_competencies.justification: The taxi-fare example modelled as an AP and the bouncing-ball height modelled as a GP both use tables and ordered-pair plots to represent a real situation and read off conclusions, such as the kilometre at which a fare is reached or the bounce after which the ball drops below a target height.

Deriving the sum of the first n natural numbers by pairing terms forwards and backwards, and connecting the resulting formula to the triangular-number sequence, extends the student's facility with the structure and properties of the natural and real numbers used throughout the progression formulas.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to generate and predict the terms of a sequence by analysing whether the rule governing it is explicit or recursive and turning that analysis into a working procedure for any progression.
